diagnosticQuestions.prompt: In one sentence, what is a share of stock?
choices: A fast, scary drop in prices across many stocks. It hurts in the moment, but history shows markets have recovered after major crashes — though it can take time.
A share is a small slice of ownership in a company. If the company does well over time, your slice can become more valuable.
Because hype moves fast and emotions run hot. Beginners do better building boring basics: learn terms, diversify, think in years, and avoid gambling with money you need.

teachSlides.title: In one sentence, what is a share of stock?
teachSlides.body:
A share is a small slice of ownership in a company. If the company does well over time, your slice can become more valuable.

**Example:** Imagine you and nine friends open a lemonade stand and split it 10 ways. Each person’s paper slip is like one share — you own part of the business.

teachSlides.title: Why do companies sell stock to the public?
teachSlides.body:
They sell pieces of ownership to raise money so they can grow — hire people, build factories, invent products, or pay down debt.

**Example:** A bike company needs $1 million for a new factory. Instead of borrowing it all from a bank, it sells shares so many investors chip in.

teachSlides.title: What does it mean that the stock market has “gone up on average” over many years?
teachSlides.body:
It means that, measured over long stretches (like decades), broad groups of stocks have tended to grow faster than cash sitting in a drawer — even though there are crashes along the way.

**Example:** Think of a bumpy road that still climbs a hill: short parts go down (crashes), but the long trip is upward if you zoom out far enough.

teachSlides.title: What is inflation, in simple words?
teachSlides.body:
Inflation is when money buys less over time because prices for food, rent, and other stuff slowly rise.

**Example:** If a movie ticket was $8 last year and $9 this year, your same $8 buys less fun than before.

teachSlides.title: Why can keeping all your savings as cash for decades be risky?
teachSlides.body:
Because inflation can shrink what your money can buy, even if the number on your account stays the same.

**Example:** $1,000 under your mattress is still $1,000 — but years later it might only buy what $600 used to buy.

teachSlides.title: What is the basic difference between saving and investing?
teachSlides.body:
Saving is parking money somewhere safer and steadier (like a bank account). Investing means taking risk to try to grow money over time, usually by buying assets like stocks or funds.

**Example:** Savings account: small, steady interest. Stocks: bigger possible growth, but the value can swing up and down a lot.

teachSlides.title: Do you need to be rich to start learning about investing?
teachSlides.body:
No. Many people start small and learn habits first — regular amounts, patience, and not betting money they need for rent or food.

**Example:** Like practicing piano 10 minutes a day: small, steady steps beat waiting for a “perfect” moment.

teachSlides.title: What does “long-term” usually mean for stock investing?
teachSlides.body:
It means measuring results in years or decades, not days — giving good companies and diversified baskets time to ride out bad years.

**Example:** Planting a tree: you do not dig it up every week to check roots. You water it and wait seasons.

teachSlides.title: What is a stock market crash?
teachSlides.body:
A fast, scary drop in prices across many stocks. It hurts in the moment, but history shows markets have recovered after major crashes — though it can take time.

**Example:** Like a sudden thunderstorm on a hike: unpleasant, but not the same as the trail disappearing forever.

teachSlides.title: Why is copying “whatever is hype on the internet today” a bad plan for beginners?
teachSlides.body:
Because hype moves fast and emotions run hot. Beginners do better building boring basics: learn terms, diversify, think in years, and avoid gambling with money you need.

**Example:** Chasing a viral stock tip is like buying concert tickets from a stranger in a parking lot — sometimes fine, often a mess.

recapSlide.title: ⚡ Level 1 — Beginner
recapSlide.body:
What stocks are, why people invest, and how the market behaves over long periods.

Start with the first card anytime you need a reset.
